one_line_takeaway: A proven mathematical flaw in weighted-average multi-turn risk scoring (bounded by the max per-turn score, so persistence never crosses threshold) is fixed with an additive peak+persistence+diversity formula that hits 90.8% recall at 1.20% FPR on a 10,654-conversation corpus, but the approach is explicitly limited to regex-detectable injection phrasing and cannot catch Crescendo-style topic-trajectory jailbreaks (0% recall on SafeMTData/hh-rlhf).
key_finding: On a 10,654-conversation holdout evaluation corpus (588 attacks + 10,066 benign), peak+accumulation scoring achieves 90.8% recall (534/588 true positives, 54 false negatives), 81.5% precision, 85.9% F1, 1.20% false positive rate (121/10,066 false positives, 9,945 true negatives), and 98.4% accuracy at rho=0.45, tau=0.7. Per-dataset accuracy: 9/9 (100%) on handcrafted multi-turn attacks, 525/579 (90.7%) on WildJailbreak-sourced attacks, 6/6 (100%) on handcrafted benign, 60/60 (100%) on WildJailbreak sparse-benign (isolated single injected turn among benign turns -- correctly not flagged), 9,879/10,000 (98.8%) on WildChat benign. A sensitivity sweep over the persistence factor rho reveals a phase transition between rho=0.375 (77.4% recall) and rho=0.400 (89.8% recall), a 12.4-point recall jump for only a 0.08pp FPR increase (1.11% to 1.19%); the paper attributes this to weight-0.3 categories (escalation_probing, deferred_authority) crossing the 0.7 threshold simultaneously once match_ratio=1.0 conversations satisfy 0.3+rho >= 0.7. The chosen default rho=0.45 gives F1=85.9% (reported as the peak); rho=0.5 gives 91.8% recall/1.28% FPR and rho=0.6 gives 95.9% recall/1.51% FPR, with the paper describing diminishing marginal recall-per-FPR beyond rho=0.5. The 54 false negatives are attributed to 39 single-category conversations using only low-weight categories (escalation_probing/deferred_authority, weight 0.3) with match ratios too low to cross threshold, plus 15 escalation-gradient conversations diluted by benign opening turns. The 121 false positives are attributed by the authors to regex pattern specificity (roleplay phrasing, legitimate AI-capability discussion, creative writing with authority figures) rather than to the scoring formula itself. On out-of-scope datasets SafeMTData (crescendo-style) and Anthropic hh-rlhf (social engineering), both the weighted-average and peak+accumulation formulas scored 0% recall, which the paper attributes to deliberately innocuous attack language that requires semantic/topic-trajectory detection beyond regex capability; these results were excluded from the headline evaluation.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is a deterministic, non-LLM proxy-level formula ('peak + accumulation scoring') for aggregating per-turn regex-based risk scores into a conversation-level risk score for multi-turn LLM prompt-injection/jailbreak detection. The authors first prove (Theorem 1, 'weighted-average ceiling') that the intuitive recency-weighted average of per-turn scores is mathematically bounded by the maximum per-turn score and therefore cannot detect persistent low-score attacks regardless of conversation length. They then propose an additive formula: score = clamp(peak + match_ratio*rho + diversity + escalation_bonus + resampling_bonus, 0, 1), where peak is the max per-turn score, match_ratio is the fraction of turns with any pattern match, diversity rewards matches spanning multiple attack-pattern categories, and two bonuses fire on detected escalation gradients (3+ turns of strictly increasing scores) or resampling (3+ consecutive user-message pairs with trigram Jaccard similarity > 0.5). Per-turn scores themselves come from a 5-category regex pattern library (instruction_seeding, role_confusion, deferred_authority, escalation_probing, repetition_resampling) with fixed weights. The method is implemented as an L4 layer in an open-source Rust HTTP proxy firewall called Parapet, and the paper releases the algorithm, pattern library, and evaluation harness.
threat_model: Deployment setting: an LLM API proxy/firewall sitting between client and model endpoint that must make allow/block decisions without invoking any LLM (to avoid added latency of 100-500ms, inference cost, and the risk of prompt-injecting the classifier itself). Attacker: distributes malicious intent for prompt injection or jailbreak across multiple conversation turns so that no single turn crosses a per-turn detection threshold, exploiting proxies/detectors that evaluate each turn independently. The paper explicitly scopes out attacks that use deliberately innocuous language with topic-trajectory escalation (e.g., Crescendo-style, content-safety jailbreaks) as out-of-scope for a regex/proxy-level approach, stating this requires LLM-based semantic classification and is a limitation of the proxy-level approach generally, not of their scoring formula specifically.
limitations: Limitations stated by the paper itself: (1) the approach is fundamentally unable to detect content-safety/topic-trajectory attacks (e.g., Crescendo) that use no injection-like phraseology, since it depends entirely on regex pattern matches -- 0% recall on SafeMTData and hh-rlhf; (2) the 588-conversation WildJailbreak-derived attack corpus is synthetically composed (real WildJailbreak adversarial sentences assembled into multi-turn sequences by the authors' own strategies), not a naturally occurring multi-turn attack dataset, because the paper states no public multi-turn prompt-injection dataset exists; (3) regex patterns are inherently brittle to rephrasing, encoding tricks, and indirect phrasing, a known general limitation of pattern-based defenses the authors explicitly attribute to a cited source rather than claim to solve; (4) the false positives observed are attributed to pattern specificity issues rather than scoring-formula issues, meaning real-world FPR depends heavily on the (unevaluated for robustness) regex library quality; (5) the min_user_turns=2 activation threshold and the layered defense-in-depth positioning (L0/L3/L4/L5a) mean this formula is only one component and its standalone real-world performance outside Parapet is untested.

datasets_used: Handcrafted multi-turn attacks (9 cases, 5 attack categories, self-constructed)

WildJailbreak attacks (579/588 cases, multi-turn conversations synthetically composed from WildJailbreak's 262K adversarial/benign prompt pairs; 5,529 unique injection sentences extracted across 4 L4 categories from 82K adversarial prompts)
WildJailbreak sparse-benign (60 cases, one injected turn among 3-4 benign turns)
WildChat benign (10,000 cases sampled from the 838K-conversation WildChat dataset, filtered for 3+ user turns all marked non-toxic)
SafeMTData (out-of-scope, crescendo-style content-safety attacks, excluded from headline results, 0% recall)
Anthropic hh-rlhf (out-of-scope, social-engineering attacks, excluded from headline results, 0% recall)
quantitative_results: Overall holdout (10,654 conversations, rho=0.45, tau=0.7): 90.8% recall, 81.5% precision, 85.9% F1, 1.20% FPR, 98.4% accuracy
True positives 534/588, false negatives 54/588, false positives 121/10,066, true negatives 9,945/10,066

Per-dataset accuracy: handcrafted attacks 9/9 (100%), WildJailbreak attacks 525/579 (90.7%), handcrafted benign 6/6 (100%), WildJailbreak sparse-benign 60/60 (100%), WildChat benign 9,879/10,000 (98.8%)
Weighted-average baseline on 9 handcrafted attacks: 5/9 scored 0.3-0.5, below the 0.7 threshold (all incorrectly allowed)
Sensitivity sweep: phase transition between rho=0.375 (77.4% recall, 1.11% FPR) and rho=0.400 (89.8% recall, 1.19% FPR)
rho=0.5: 91.8% recall, 1.28% FPR; rho=0.6: 95.9% recall, 1.51% FPR
Out-of-scope datasets (SafeMTData, hh-rlhf): 0% recall under both weighted-average and peak+accumulation formulas
baselines_compared: Weighted (recency-biased) average of per-turn scores -- the paper's own strawman/prior-intuitive baseline, proven mathematically bounded by max per-turn score (Theorem 1) and empirically shown to miss 5/9 handcrafted persistent attacks

No external ML/LLM baseline classifiers were run head-to-head; LLM-based multi-turn detectors (Defensive M2S, MindGuard, AprielGuard) are discussed only as related work with their own reported numbers (e.g., Defensive M2S 93.8% recall, cited from that paper), not re-evaluated on this paper's corpus
